title: The Algebra of Free Fermions: Classifying Spaces, Hamiltonians, and Computation
abstract: Research on topological phases of matter is a core field in modern condensed matter physics. Free fermion systems, such as topological insulators and superconductors, have been studied using the "Tenfold Way" and K-theory. Building on Kitaev's idea of $Ω$-spectrum and classifying space, as well as Freed-Moore's K-theory, this work demonstrates that free fermionic systems form a genuine $G$-$Ω$-spectrum and clarifies its connection to several distinct classification schemes appearing in the physical literature. By introducing the $\mathbb{Z}_2$-graded algebra $A_{\mathrm{sym}}^V$, the classification problem for systems with general symmetries, including antilinear symmetries, antisymmetries, projective representations, and point group symmetries, is turned into an extension problem in representation theory. To solve this, a computational method for the $\mathbb{Z}_2$-graded Wedderburn-Artin decomposition of $A_{\mathrm{sym}}^V$ is developed. This decomposition not only yields a classification but also enables the explicit construction of the corresponding Dirac Hamiltonian. Furthermore, a GAP programming package has been developed to automate these calculations.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous mathematical framework for classifying topological phases of free fermions under general symmetries. By reducing the topological problem to an algebraic decomposition problem, the authors enable the explicit construction of model Dirac Hamiltonians and provide a computational tool for these calculations.
main_problem: The paper seeks a systematic topological classification of gapped free fermionic systems (like topological insulators and superconductors) under complex symmetries, including crystalline, magnetic, and antilinear symmetries.
main_result: The authors prove that free fermionic systems form a genuine G-Omega-spectrum and establish an isomorphism between the algebraic classification via Wedderburn-Artin decomposition and the topological classification via K-theory.
method: The work utilizes an algebraic approach involving Z2-graded algebras, Clifford algebras, and the Wedderburn-Artin decomposition, supplemented by a developed GAP software package for automated computation.
model_or_system: The study focuses on (q+1)-dimensional gapped free fermionic systems, specifically modeling Dirac Hamiltonians and their mass terms within a framework of G-equivariant vector bundles over a representation sphere.
key_observables: Topological invariants, Frobenius-Schur indicators, and the structure of the division algebra D.
important_parameters: Symmetry group G, 2-cocycles (omega), and the Z2-grading of the algebra.
main_assumptions: The classification focuses on strong topological phases and assumes the existence of an energy gap to partition the Hilbert space.
paper_structure: The paper moves from defining the symmetry algebra and classifying spaces to establishing the theoretical foundation for computation, followed by the implementation of the GAP package and an appendix with explicit examples.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
